q34: Over the past year, we've implemented a new automated Section 508 checklist to help speed up procurement of new ICT.  The checklist is built into the procurement system used for acquisitions, so there is no skipping around 508. Buyers have to submit a 508 checklist for all new ICT procurements.  We also tested our top ten websites, internal pages, documents and videos, including website templates and data visualizations in an effort to improve the user experience of our main webpage.
q35: Our Section 508 service contract was not renewed and we lost contract support that provided training, helped test our ICT products and documents, and conducted research on accessible emerging technology and trends.  To help overcome the lost, we created testing instructions that product owners can use to self test their products and ensure compliance before release.  We've also beefed up training and learning sessions with internal development teams to make them more aware of Section 508 and common mistakes content creators make.  Finally, we updated our 508 policy in alignment with the new administration's 508 goals.
q36: It would be great to have a federal repository that contained known findings of ICT products. Federal agencies could help build the repository by contributing content or findings when products are tested and evaluated.  It would be informational only and not the holy grail, unless someone entity was responsible for vetting the content. Also, it would be great to have an automated web scanning tool for 508 testing that GSA/OMB sponsored, similar to Social Security and the ANDI tool.  Agencies are all over the board with tools, so consolidating test tools would help with efficiency, cost and enable agencies to compare apples to apples when evaluating products.

q16aother: I serve as the NIST Section 508 compliance manager on a part-time basis, as this role is not my primary position. To ensure I am knowledgeable about 508 standards and accessibility, I have committed to extensive training. In fiscal year 2026, all new and current web content creators will also be required to complete 508/accessibility training.
q34:
The NIST Public Affairs Office recently produced a training video outlining best practices for creating 508-compliant websites. This training will soon be mandatory for all web content authors.

One of our agency's biggest challenges is remediating legacy PDFs. Over the past year, the Section 508 program has worked with various NIST organizational units to introduce them to the DOC's Section 508 Services Blanket Purchasing Agreement. This agreement allows them to obtain contractor services for document remediation, among other services, ensuring they meet 508 compliance and accessibility standards.

As the Section 508 Program Manager, I can only dedicate approximately 10 hours a week to this role; however, I have become more proactive over the past year in identifying 508 and accessibility-related issues with both internal and public-facing digital content.  I regularly reached out to authors and publishers to address these issues.  Given that our organization has over 5,000 staff members, it is not feasible for me to oversee all digital content created across the bureau. Nevertheless, I have made significant efforts to engage more frequently with content creators, in addition to responding to any inquiries or requests for assistance they may have.
q35:
Amid budget constraints, we were unable to renew our Siteimprove license, which was terminated in April.  Siteimprove provided several important benefits related to Section 508 compliance and accessibility, including:  
•        Automated accessibility testing  
•        Real-time monitoring that alerted users to new accessibility issues  
•        Comprehensive reporting that identified accessibility violations and offered recommendations for remediation.  

Having an automated tool for testing, monitoring, and reporting would greatly benefit the agency by supporting continuous improvement in our Section 508 compliance efforts.

In addition, I serve as the NIST Section 508 program manager on a part-time basis only, as this is not my primary position.  As a result, it is challenging to fulfill the responsibilities of the 508 PM.  In the future, when the budget allows, the best solution would be to hire a full-time, dedicated Section 508 program manager.
q36: I would like to note that we were able to use Siteimprove for automated testing, etc., for most of this reporting period, but we were recently unable to renew the license.

q10resource: Assist acquisition officials to prepare Section 508 language in ICT contracts,Assist acquisition officials to evaluate Section 508 conformance prior to or after ICT purchase,Assist developers in creating web content or software that complies with Section 508,Create or remediate electronic documents to comply with Section 508,Evaluate and/or remediate web content,Provide training
q34: Over the past year, our agency has made significant strides in strengthening our commitment to digital accessibility. A pivotal development was the establishment of a dedicated, full-time Section 508 Program Manager to provide focused leadership and strategic oversight for our accessibility initiatives. To enhance our technical capabilities, we successfully implemented the Siteimprove accessibility monitoring platform agency-wide, providing automated conformance testing for over 250 websites. This tool empowers our teams with actionable insights to identify and remediate barriers. Complementing these technical advancements, we launched a new curriculum of training on Section 508 conformance, ensuring our staff has the knowledge to create accessible content. These efforts were amplified by special training events, expertly prepared and hosted by the OCIO Digital Program, further embedding accessibility best practices throughout the agency.
q35: Our agency faces significant challenges in achieving full Section 508 conformance, a task the OCIO Digital Program began tackling from the ground up just last year. The primary hurdle is the daunting scope of our digital footprint, which includes hundreds of websites, each containing dozens, if not hundreds, of electronic files. A substantial portion of this content hasn't been reviewed for compliance since the standards were updated to incorporate WCAG in 2018, creating a significant remediation backlog. This complexity is compounded by the highly technical nature of our scientific information, which requires subject matter experts for accurate accessibility testing. These issues are magnified by extremely limited resources for monitoring and remediation, with most compliance work currently performed by employees as a collateral duty on top of their full-time responsibilities, making systematic progress incredibly difficult.
q36: On behalf of our agency, I would like to extend our sincere thanks to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) and the General Services Administration (GSA) for your continued efforts and partnership on this annual assessment. This process is invaluable; it helps drive our agency to improve our compliance efforts and gives a powerful voice to the importance of meeting this federal statute. Your work is a critical catalyst for change and helps us champion the resources needed to ensure our digital services are accessible to all.

q34: DeCA is looking to stand up a formalized Section 508 program.  The agency will conduct a systematic review of its websites, documents, and other electronic communication tools identify and remediate compliance issues.  The agency is looking to authorize FTE personnel in the future that will be the designated agency POCs for this program.
q35:
Over the past year, DeCA has encountered several challenges in achieving full Section 508 compliance:
1. Limited Staffing and Expertise: As a small agency, we have a limited number of staff with formal training in accessibility standards. This has made it difficult to consistently create, evaluate, and remediate digital content for 508 compliance.
2. Inconsistent Accessibility Reviews: Due to resource constraints, accessibility reviews are not conducted in the development process or inconsistently across projects, increasing the likelihood of non-compliant deliverables.
3. Lack of Centralized Guidance and Tools: There is no standardized, agency-wide process or toolkit for ensuring accessibility, leading to varying levels of compliance across different teams and content types.
4. Third-Party Content and Software: Several tools and platforms used by the agency—especially third-party applications and websites have not been tested for 508 compliancy, and we have no staff with formal training to test and enforce accessibility improvements.
q36: A Department of Defense directive enforcing the establishment of a formal Section 508 program, including the allocation of dedicated full-time equivalents (FTEs), would significantly enhance DeCA's ability to prioritize and allocate the necessary resources to ensure compliance with accessibility requirements.

q34:
* Just completed Agency 508 Policy, now going through the approval process.
* Integrated axe Linter Server into DLA's Development pipeline to enable early, automated accessibility checks during development.
* Expanded use of axe Monitor for 424 DLA applications to support enterprise-wide WCAG 2.0 AA compliance testing, identifying 50 to 55% non-conformance rates.  Remaining rates are to be done with manual testing.
* Developed a repeatable Section 508 compliance process that will evaluate, remediate, validate, and approve DLA's web and applications compliance.  Subject to PEO/CIO approval and then implementation to DLA's workforce.
* All accomplishments:
  * Improves consistency and accountability
  * Reduces risk and non-compliance
  * Enhances efficiency
  * Supports mission readiness
  * Enables informed decision-making
q35:
Section 508 compliance is an important area that often gets overlooked and can lead to significant inefficiencies, especially in IT procurement, digital communications, and service delivery.

One significant area of inefficiency in the Defense Logistic Agency is the inconsistent implementation and enforcement of Section 508 compliance for digital services and IT systems.  Despite clear federal mandates, many software systems, internal tools, websites, and electronic documents are deployed without undergoing proper 508 compliance testing.  This results in retroactive remediation costs, legal risks, and a lack of accessibility for employees and stakeholders with disabilities.

For example, acquisition processes often fail to include Section 508 requirements from the start, and critical IT systems are purchased or developed without ensuring they meet accessibility standards.  Similarly, digital documents, SharePoint environments, and training materials are routinely released without the necessary structure or metadata to work with assistive technologies.  These gaps could result in duplicated efforts, contract delays, and reduced workforce productivity.

Suggested Fix:  Establish and mandate Section 508 Senior Leadership positions within the component with direct support to Component Director or CIO.  This designated official would be responsible for setting policy, overseeing compliance, managing 508 Program Manager, and ensuring accountability across the agency.  By placing 508 oversight at the strategic leadership level, the DLA can move from a reactive to a proactive model--integrating accessibility at the start of every IT, procurement, and communication initiative.  This change would not only ensure compliance but also foster inclusivity, reduce long-term costs, and strengthen mission effectiveness across the enterprise.

q34: In FY24, DTRA has increased from zero certified testers to 11, with four additional in the program utilizing DHS’s Trusted Tester Program. Additionally, DTRA implemented a hybrid testing approach utilizing free and existing resources in the software development process and on intranet/internet sites by locally hosting and offering manual testing tools (ANDI and ACRT (SCRT)) and Compliance Guardian. DTRA created a tester community of practice which is a forum that consists of system owners, testers, and stakeholders across the Agency. The focus is to collaborate on lessons-learned, best practices, challenges, and to cross-feed ideas. Additionally, DTRA now offers ‘Introduction to Section 508’ training for all DTRA personnel via Defense Acquisition University Learning Management System (DAU LMS). This is in addition to our CIO Acquisition training, which is required for PM certification. The S508 program office launched an awareness campaign across the component utilizing digital signage, stakeholder engagement, and by providing formal and informal training to stakeholders. Developed additional acquisition processes to identify and remediate ICT prior to acquisition. Lastly, the S508 PO published a revised version of DTRA’s accessibility policy, DTRAI 1020.02 implementing OMB Memorandum 24-08 Strengthening Digital Accessibility and the Management of Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act.

q34:
In the past year, the Army has significantly strengthened its commitment to Section 508 compliance through a series of strategic implementations and initiatives. For the first time, the Army has appointed a full-time Section 508 Program Manager. This dedicated leadership role centralizes oversight and strategic direction for all compliance efforts, indicating a heightened institutional priority for accessibility.
We have established a specialized team that includes two individuals certified as DHS Trusted Testers for web applications. This certification ensures that our web application testing adheres to a standardized, rigorous methodology, leading to more accurate and reliable compliance assessments.
We have developed and implemented comprehensive internal SOPs for testing both documents and web applications. These standardized procedures provide clear guidelines for our teams, promoting consistency and thoroughness in identifying and addressing accessibility barriers. We have initiated the development of targeted Section 508 training modules. These programs are designed to equip Army personnel with the knowledge and skills necessary to create, procure, and maintain accessible ICT from the outset.
We have actively begun engaging with Disability Program Managers (DPMs) to directly address and assist with assistive technology challenges faced by individuals with disabilities. This collaboration ensures that solutions are  responsive to real-time needs. We are conducting regular, systematic audits of both applications and documents to proactively identify and rectify compliance gaps. These ongoing assessments provide critical insights into our current accessibility posture and drive continuous improvement.
 We are actively developing new policies and processes to integrate Section 508 compliance into critical operational areas. These changes collectively represent a significant leap forward in the Army's commitment to creating a more  accessible digital environment for all its personnel and the public.
q35:
The Army faced considerable challenges in achieving consistent Section 508 compliance over the past year, primarily due to its vast size. A significant obstacle has been a widespread lack of awareness regarding the Section 508 Program Office's authority and its crucial role. However, despite these hurdles, the Army Section 508 Program Office has been proactive with developing guidance and policies and establishing working groups to address challenges and increase awareness. Our tiger team has been actively addressing Assistive Technology challenges amidst the Army's large-scale modernization efforts. We've also undertaken strategic communication with Army Staff to boost program visibility and overcome awareness gaps.
To ensure consistent oversight, we've developed internal processes and procedures that are repeatable even at the small unit level. These proactive measures are transforming Army Section 508 compliance from afterthought into an embedded practice. This ensures equitable access for all and strengthens the Army's overall mission readiness.

q34: The changes implemented in the past year to increase Section 508 compliance for OCJCS PA were the corrected issues on the JCS.mil website identified by the DCIO Resources and Analysis team in March 2025. JS DOM ESSD supported the DISA-led content migration to DOD365-Joint (IL5) tenant and provided functional support for Joint Staff users to implement content and collaboration in the modern M365 and SharePoint Online environment. Usability and accessibility features are integrated into the DOD365-Joint tenant's service offering and support "alt" tags, custom metadata for lists and libraries, enterprise metadata, and simplified page and site architecture for content discovery. SharePoint applications and process support workflows have been remediated from InfoPath forms to simplified list-based, native forms without custom scripting and simplified logic for rules and alerts. JS Comptroller had mandatory EEO and RA Training.

The implementation of Section 508 functional support will be better supported with the conclusion of the migration of Joint Staff SharePoint sites and transition into greater focus on implementation of governance and content management to support collaboration and records management. OCJCS PA faced the lack of clear 508 compliance instructions and training, as well as the need to identify key personnel to focus their time and effort on 508 compliance, instead of additional duties tacked on to personnel already tasked with more timely and pressing responsibilities.

q34:
Over the past year, the NSA has implemented several key changes to bolster Section 508 compliance. We have significantly enhanced our outreach, training, and engagement efforts through the following initiatives:

Revamped Program Management Reviews (PMRs): We have redesigned the PMR reporting process to improve its effectiveness and efficiency. This includes validating accessibility scoring methods to ensure they accurately reflect the accessibility status of our technologies.

Expanded Geographical Reach: To extend our impact beyond the NSAW Headquarters, we have established sub-programs in both Hawaii and Colorado. This expansion allows us to provide more targeted support and resources to these regions.

Leveraged Emerging Technologies: We have begun integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) into certain aspects of the IT Accessibility Scoresheet. By automating specific tasks, we aim to increase accuracy, expedite the scoring process, and allow our team to focus on more complex accessibility evaluations.

Enhanced Training and Awareness: To foster a culture of accessibility, we have conducted numerous training sessions and workshops. These events aim to educate and empower our workforce to prioritize and implement Section 508 compliance in their respective roles.

These strategic changes demonstrate our commitment to improving IT accessibility and ensuring that our technologies can be effectively used by all employees.
q35:
In the past year, the NSA has faced a few challenges with regard to Section 508 compliance. Of note:

Verification and Enforcement in Acquisition and Procurement: Ensuring that all acquired and procured technologies adhere to accessibility standards has proven to be challenging. Current processes may not always sufficiently verify compliance, which can lead to the acquisition of non-compliant IT products and services.

To overcome this challenge and further mature our Section 508 compliance program, the following would be beneficial:

Strengthened Acquisition and Procurement Procedures: Enhancing our acquisition policies and procedures to include more rigorous verification steps would help enforce compliance. Additionally, providing accessibility training to procurement personnel could improve their ability to evaluate and ensure the compliance of potential acquisitions.

q34:
We have made several impactful improvements to help ACL program offices and their supporting contractors make their ICT Section 508 and WCAG compliant. We created and shared presentations with the ACL community via Microsoft Teams and disseminated helpful accessibility training information to system owners via accessibility reports. 

A 508 Team Lead was appointed to manage and oversee the Section 508 program and provide support to ACL program offices and ACL OIRM contractors. We also developed an easy-to-follow library of helpful resources on accessibility techniques, guidelines, requirements, and best practices that we share with the ACL community. We encourage supporting contractors to take advantage of free online Section 508 training courses offered by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
q35:
Over the past year, we’ve faced several challenges in fully implementing and sustaining Section 508 compliance efforts. One of the primary issues has been a lack of dedicated resources, both in terms of staffing and accessibility tools. Due to budget constraints, we currently have a limited number of contractors and federal employees who are trained and available to support our Section 508 program, which has made it challenging to meet the growing demand for accessibility reviews and customer training.

Another ongoing challenge has been the limited selection of accessibility tools available on the HHS-approved software list, which has restricted our ability to fully assess and remediate ICT using the most effective technologies. While the HHS automated web crawler tool is expected to resolve some of these issues, it hasn’t been implemented by the department yet.

An increase in software tools and resource support would help us meet the increasing demand for 508 and WCAG accessibility reviews and training.

•        Developed an automated tool for tracking end-to-end processes for Section 508.
•        Created an issues tracking tool for agency use.
•        Implemented an agency-wide process for collecting, remediating, and adjudicating accessibility-related issues raised by CMS staff.
•        Established an Accessibility Executive Steering Committee composed of senior and management staff overseeing accessibility-related areas.

•        Obtained external document remediation services designed to increase turnaround times for distributing accessible documents.

q34: n FY25, NIH developed new virtual instructor-led training modules on specific Section 508 topics; revamped the internal Section 508 website with updated how-to guides and job aids; established an acquisitions working group and drafted SOPs to better integrate Section 508 into the procurement process; developed an internal ACR creation tool using GSA’s open-source OPENACR Editor; commissioned an AI chatbot trained on Section 508 and NIH policy content; updated the LMS Section 508 Awareness training; and revised IT Support SOPs to incorporate accessibility requirements.
q35: In FY25, NIH experienced significant resource loss due to staffing reductions, particularly across communications, EEO, and acquisitions teams, resulting in a 75% drop in sub-component Section 508 support. This impacted our ability to review acquisitions for compliance and required revision of draft SOPs. A major challenge is the lack of consistent, government-wide guidance on how to incorporate Section 508 into acquisition processes. NIH would benefit from increased Section 508 resources, standardized acquisition guidance, and expanded support and shared services coordinated through GSA to improve consistency and reduce duplication across agencies.
q36: NIH is committed to advancing Section 508 compliance and would welcome greater investment in shared services and AI-driven tools provided through GSA. We see strong potential for generative AI and LLMs to support agencies by automating accessibility evaluations, drafting ACRs, auditing documentation, and assisting with acquisition reviews. Centralized, secure tools developed and maintained through Section508.gov could significantly reduce agency burden and promote consistency across the federal enterprise.

q34:
1. The Management Directive was updated to expand the roles and responsibilities of HQ’s Section 508 program.  The updates included alignment to ADA law and executive order 24-08, additional service support for the Components and an increased focus on usability. 
2. Six questions were added to the DHS’ Program Health Assessment (PHA), which allow DHS to identify how major programs are implement Section 508 compliance into products and services.  PHA reviews also allows DHS to identify hoe it is maturing  its programs ability to provide Section 508 compliant solutions. 
3. Updated the Trusted Tester program to reduce the time it takes individuals to become certified Trusted Testers.  The time to complete the course on average was reduced from 80 hours to 60 hours. Additionally, modifications to the course has improved people’s success rate for passing the certification exam on the first attempt.    
4. Microsoft Office Word, Excel, PowerPoint and Adobe Pro electronic document accessibility courses were update to align with changes made to Microsoft Office 365 and the latest cloud version of Acrobat. 
5. 508 compliance contract language that goes into all Statement of Work (SOWs) was improved for readability and clarity. 
6.  508 compliance requirements were added to the SLC and SDLC (Software Design Lifecycle).
q35: The Department is currently going through restructuring which is making it difficult to identify and coordinate with key stakeholders.  Additionally, HQ does not have full access to the Components’ internal systems and HQ lacks access to some of the Components’ websites.  To get around these issue, HQ has to either establish a MOA with a Component or put a staff member on detail.  It would be beneficial to identify a simple way to grant access to systems and websites without having to place a person on detail.

q34:
- Section 508 now has items in the DHS OIG SIP which is a primary document for organizational goals and objectives 
- We added a full time contractor for web inspections. 
- We have done document re-inspections on all documents that were put out to our Public website in the past few years before our expertise was perfected. 
- We have performed a full Public site web inspection and are remediating those discoveries. 
- We have initiated web inspections on internal websites. 
- We have built Web Inspection Issue trackers to help ensure that all issues detected are remediated.  
- We have worked on establishing Section 508 templates to be used within the OIG for report and document construction.
- We have further evolved our internal Section 508 website. 
- This was supposed to be the FY that the OIG had its first mandatory basic 508 course assignment for all staff, but that was delayed another year.
q35:
The DHS OIG Digital Accessibility group is still an informal unofficial organization. This means that much of the "agenda" that we would like to pursue 
has to be done in informal "back-channel" types of ways. We can advise and assist but do not have the authority to dictate or act proactively in any public way. 
The DHS OIG has recently gone to the MS365 technology stack, the proper ways to inspect applications on that platform are still being learned. We are already being told that the issues we detected are built into the technology and cannot be fixed by developers. Training of web and application developers has also been quite challenging.

q36: OIG primarily issues reports and other similar documents, which are publicly posted. Given the nature of our responsibilities, OIG does not have a standalone program. However, OIG is aware of and strives to comply with our 508 related responsibilities. We ensure that all required information is made 508 compliant and accessible to people with disabilities, including both employees and the public we serve. To do so, OIG currently retains a contractor to assist in making our reports and other documents 508 compliant, and it is an established part of our process. Finally, we have a staff member (508 Program Manager) whose responsibilities include 508 coordination. This staff member works with the Department of Interior points of contact and elsewhere to obtain information on 508 issues. This staff member likewise assists in working with the contractor to ensure that our reports and other documents are 508 compliant. When completing this assessment, we reviewed and provided responses to these questions based on our knowledge, current resources, and our 508 compliant processes.

q34:
Over the past year, the Bureau of Engraving and Printing (BEP) has taken bold, intentional steps to elevate Section 508 compliance from a technical requirement to an enterprise-wide strategic priority. This transformation began with a comprehensive organizational assessment, a detailed gap analysis, and a Capability Maturity Model (CMM) evaluation. These assessments laid a data-informed foundation that helped guide the launch of several high-impact initiatives aligned with BEP’s operational needs and long-term mission.

Strategic Program Development and Roadmap

One of the most significant achievements was the development of a formal Accessibility Strategic Plan, complete with milestones, performance metrics, and accountability mechanisms. This plan now informs every aspect of BEP’s compliance approach—from process design to cultural change—and is integrated into the agency’s broader technology roadmap. BEP also finalized a formal IT508 Policy, institutionalized a governance framework, and implemented a risk-based remediation model that prioritizes efforts based on complexity, urgency, and public exposure. These structures, now being formalized at the OCIO level, provide consistency and strategic alignment across all accessibility decisions.

Expansion of Testing and Technical Capacity

A cornerstone of BEP’s programmatic progress is the establishment of a Trusted Tester team consisting of nine DHS-certified professionals. This internal capability ensures that all ICT assets--including hardware, software, web content, and electronic documents—are rigorously evaluated through manual and automated conformance testing. With this team in place, BEP is able to independently validate accessibility, increase accountability, and enhance technical precision at scale.

Remediation Volume and Timeliness Enhancements

In FY24, BEP processed an average of 141 documents per month, in addition to providing over 40 hours of video captioning, including numerous high and very high complexity files. A structured triage system and SLA-based turnaround standards (2, 7, and 21 business days) were enforced to improve throughput, reduce backlog, and ensure accountability. Monthly and quarterly dashboards now monitor submission volume, turnaround time, and document complexity—bringing increased visibility to program performance.

Tool and Process Expansion

To support scalable, high-quality compliance, BEP expanded its toolkit to include a full suite of industry-standard testing and remediation tools such as Microsoft Accessibility Checker, Adobe Acrobat Pro DC, CommonLook, ANDI, WAVE, Color Contrast Analyzer, JAWS, NVDA, and ZoomText. These tools enable both automated and manual testing across all document and web-based content prior to publication or deployment.

Comprehensive Training and Workforce Development

BEP launched a structured, role-specific training ecosystem to equip staff with practical accessibility skills. This includes 
•        Instructional videos, cheat sheets, and microlearning clips
•        Hands-on training manuals for screen readers (NVDA and JAWS)
•        A monthly training cadence, with twice-monthly sessions: one focused on technical developers (covering ARIA, tab indexing, and accessible interactions, ARIA roles, and dynamic content) and one on Microsoft Office Suite accessibility, covering Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and accessible PDF authoring.

These sessions equip staff with real-world skills and are reinforced with recorded sessions, quick-reference guides, and live support during weekly office hours. for real-time issue resolution.

Inclusive Testing and Internal Engagement

BEP also prioritized inclusive testing by establishing internal accessibility testing workstations equipped with assistive technologies. These environments enable voluntary usability testing by BEP employees with disabilities. Detailed training and scripted scenarios have been developed and ready to be provided to the volunteer BEP employees with disabilities to ensure meaningful, consistent feedback—supporting both compliance and usability.

UI/UX Accessibility Integration

Recognizing the role of inclusive design in accessibility, BEP developed a UI/UX Section 508 Accessibility Playbook, which embeds accessibility principles throughout the design and development lifecycle. This resource ensures that inclusive design is applied proactively from the earliest stages of digital development, including prototyping, interface testing, and deployment.

Centralized Digital Support Infrastructure

To streamline support and improve user experience, BEP launched a centralized Section 508 Accessibility Website that serves as the agency’s digital hub for guidance, training, FAQs, policy updates, and accessibility resources. This effort was further supported by the implementation of an accessibility request intake and tracking platform within Helix, enabling more efficient triage, tracking, and resolution of 508 service requests. A formal 508 Support Group was also established to foster peer collaboration, knowledge sharing, and ongoing improvement.

Measurable Impact and Cultural Shift

Together, these initiatives reflect a meaningful shift from reactive remediation to strategic enablement and digital inclusion. The program’s impact is evident through improved SLA adherence, expanded testing capacity, and increasingly positive stakeholder feedback. Performance dashboards and satisfaction surveys show near-perfect on-time delivery and high levels of engagement from internal teams and leadership.

BEP’s approach to Section 508 compliance has evolved into a model of federal leadership—Strong management team from BEP’s OCIO, anchored in strategic governance, empowered by skilled teams, and reinforced by scalable processes and tools. Through its comprehensive efforts, BEP is not only meeting its federal compliance obligations, it is leading by example in building a digitally inclusive federal agency and raising the bar in accessibility across government.
q35:
While the Bureau of Engraving and Printing (BEP) has made considerable progress in advancing Section 508 compliance, several challenges remain that are consistent with the scale and complexity of building a sustainable, enterprise-wide accessibility program.

BEP is actively engaged in creating the foundational framework, designing end-to-end workflows, and developing technical guidance, processes, and tools to support accessibility across all digital assets. However, like many federal components, this work is ongoing and inherently complex. Success requires more than technical fixes; it demands coordinated process development, sustained resource investment, and a significant cultural shift across the organization.

One of the core challenges is the time required to fully operationalize and scale these efforts. Accessibility is not a one-time project, it is a continuous, evolving responsibility. While BEP is in the process of developing strong policies, tools, and training programs, widespread adoption and consistent execution across all business units takes time. Building awareness, changing habits, and institutionalizing inclusive practices across hundreds of stakeholders is a gradual process.

What would be most helpful in overcoming these challenges is continued support for long-term process improvement, dedicated time for training and testing, and recognition that cultural transformation around digital accessibility cannot happen overnight. 

While much has been accomplished, meaningful change in accessibility culture and operations requires sustained effort, patience, and system-wide reinforcement.
q36:
Yes. In closing, BEP would emphasize that accessibility is not simply a compliance requirement—it is a fundamental value embedded in our mission to serve all people equitably. Our Section 508 program continues to evolve as a strategic, cross-cutting initiative that supports digital inclusion, operational excellence, and people trust.

We are proud of the strong foundation we’ve built—comprising of a dedicated management team, skilled Trusted Tester team, structured governance, robust training, and scalable tools. At the same time, we recognize that true accessibility maturity requires ongoing commitment, collaboration, and cultural transformation. BEP welcomes continued engagement with Treasury leadership and other federal partners to share knowledge, align strategies, and champion a unified, accessible digital experience across government.

We remain steadfast in our mission to lead by example and raise the standard for digital accessibility—delivering solutions that are not only compliant but truly usable for all.

•	Mass exodus of IRS personnel to include a reduction of 508 Program staff by 30% and loss of 2 support contract task order for an additional 70% cut in support personnel.

q36:
•        As a large Component, formally responding to the Department or Agency tab questions will help the Section 508 Program Manager effectively assess, augment, collaborate, report, and monitor program maturity. Although more questions would take more time, in the past, Departments had to respond to >100 questions twice annually but there was sufficient data to monitor and identify maturity gaps. 
•        Consolidating questions may help with the number of Assessment questions but it does not reduce the number of stakeholders. As a Component, there is additional time to separate, track and consolidate responses from multiple stakeholders for consolidated questions.
•        Please always provide a crosswalk of the previous year’s Assessment when making changes to the current year Assessment to help Departments and Components seamlessly report on program maturity, data trends or identify gaps for senior leadership.
•        An up-to-date Accessibility Playbook that aligns with program maturity assessments and goals will help Section 508 Program Managers develop a strategic roadmap for alignment and improved service.
•        New terms like, “adaptive devices”, should be defined and listed on the Section508.gov Glossary of Section 508 Terms Webpage, or maintain the use of official terms like “assistive technology” for consistency.
•        Thank you for providing Potential Gaps; Recommended Inclusions; and Recommended Policy Language for Policy Subtypes. Adding Policy Subtypes to Assessment questions would provide valuable insight in support of IT governance and validate accessibility requirements are incorporated into IT Lifecycle policies and procedures.

q34:
FTA is in the process of establishing the foundation for its new Section 508 Compliance Program. FTA: 

- Hired a new Section 508 Compliance Officer in May 2024. 

- Conducted a 508 Compliance Awareness Training on May 23, 2024 for the Executive Management Team and again on October 16, 2024 to the Management Council. 

- Participated in the Departments quarterly Section 508 meetings designed for Section 508 Compliance Officers.    

- Established a 508 Accessibility Working Group and held the first meeting on December 19, 2024.    

- Established the FTA Section 508 Compliance Council and held the first meeting on June 11, 2024.  

- Facilitated Section 508 Compliance Brown Bags in partnership with FHWA featuring Alternative Text and Imaging Text Training on January 16, 2025, and Color Contrast Training on February 13, 2025.  

- Created a Section 508 Compliance mailbox (FTA508Compliance@dot.gov) to for our customers to email the Section 508 Compliance Officer. 

- Created an FTA Section 508 logo and embedded it on the website and agency communications.
